questions.text: Ravila, a new CISO in a healthcare organization, is reviewing incident response records from the past several years. Ravila has determined that minor incidents were managed with too much rigor and complexity, while major incidents were not dealt with thoroughly enough. What might be the cause of this?
options: A. Lack of training for incident responders
B. Inconsistent levels of response to incidents
C. Lack of a tiered incident response plan
D. Improperly tuned SIEM use cases
questions.justification_correct: The organization has an incident response plan with one path of response for incidents of all severities. The result is that high-severity incidents are treated too lightly and low-severity incidents are treated with too much rigor. A tiered IRP assigns different response protocols based on incident severity level.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because lack of training would more likely result in subpar response to all incidents. B is incorrect because it re-describes the symptom rather than its cause. D is incorrect because SIEM tuning issues would not produce this specific disparity between high and low severity incident handling.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a valid objection for using incident response plan templates to serve as an organization's security incident response plan?
options: A. The templates will lack the specifics about business processes and technology.
B. The templates will lack the specific regulations the organization is required to comply with.
C. The templates will lack the names of specific departments and executives.
D. The templates will not specifically call on the organization's crisis response plan.
questions.justification_correct: The objection that templates will lack specific regulation names is the weakest objection because regulatory requirements can be relatively easily added to a template. This makes it the least valid objection and therefore the correct answer to this EXCEPT-style question.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because the lack of specifics about business processes and technologies is a valid objection. C is incorrect because specific department and executive names are critical for escalation and notification paths. D is incorrect because templates typically do not reference an organization's specific crisis response plan, which is a legitimate gap.

questions.text: The purpose of documenting the steps taken during the response to an actual security incident includes all of the following EXCEPT which one?
options: A. Helps the organization understand how to respond more effectively during future incidents
B. Helps the organization understand whether incident responders followed incident response procedures
C. Helps the organization understand whether the organization recovered from the incident
D. Helps the organization understand whether the incident response was compliant with applicable laws
questions.justification_correct: Documenting steps during incident response does little to help determine whether recovery was actually achieved. Key personnel directly involved in operations will know whether recovery was complete and successful, regardless of documentation.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because documentation does help improve future response effectiveness. B is incorrect because documentation does confirm whether procedures were followed. D is incorrect because documentation does help assess legal compliance, including evidence handling and notification obligations.

questions.text: Why should incident responders participate in incident response tabletop exercises?
options: A. Helps incident responders better understand incident response procedures
B. Helps incident responders find mistakes in incident response procedures
C. Helps incident responders understand how long it should take to respond to actual incidents
D. Helps incident responders memorize incident response procedures so they can respond more quickly
questions.justification_correct: Participation in tabletop exercises helps incident responders become more familiar with incident response procedures. Talking through a simulated incident and thinking about each step helps responders better understand how to perform each step and why it is needed.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because identifying mistakes is not the primary purpose of a tabletop exercise, though it may occur as a secondary benefit. C is incorrect because measuring response time is not a primary objective of tabletop exercises. D is incorrect because memorization is not an objective of tabletop exercises.

questions.text: Why should incident responders be asked to review incident response procedures?
options: A. Helps incident responders memorize incident response procedures so they can respond more quickly
B. Helps incident responders understand how long it should take to respond to actual incidents
C. Helps incident responders better understand incident response procedures
D. Helps incident responders find mistakes in incident response procedures
questions.justification_correct: A primary purpose of IRP document review is to identify errors in response plans so that they can be corrected prior to an actual incident. Finding and fixing mistakes before an incident occurs is the principal objective of this review activity.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because memorization is not an objective of document review. B is incorrect because estimating response time is not an objective of document review. C is incorrect because while improved familiarity is a valuable by-product of document review, it is not the primary objective.

questions.text: James, the CISO in an organization, has reviewed the organization's incident response plans and disaster recovery plans and has determined that incident response plans do not include any provisions should a security incident occur during a declared disaster. What is James's most appropriate response?
options: A. Declare a security incident.
B. Request that the next tabletop exercise take place at the emergency operations center.
C. No response is required because security incident response plans are not required for DR sites.
D. Request that incident response and disaster recovery teams update the IRPs to include procedures during emergency operations mode.
questions.justification_correct: The IRP must be updated to include procedures, contact information, and other relevant information to assist responders should a security incident occur while the organization is in emergency operations mode. Both environments must be covered.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because there is no incident occurring — this is a planning gap, not an active incident. B is incorrect because a tabletop at the EOC will be ineffective without updated IRP procedures for that context. C is incorrect because security incidents can and do occur at DR sites and must be addressed by the IRP.

questions.text: Which step in an incident response plan is associated with tabletop exercises?
options: A. Remediation
B. Detection
C. Analysis
D. Planning
questions.justification_correct: Security incident response tabletop exercises are part of the planning phase. The exercise itself simulates most steps of an actual incident so that responders become more familiar with response procedures before a real event occurs.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because tabletop exercises are not limited to the remediation step. B is incorrect because tabletop exercises are not limited to the detection step. C is incorrect because tabletop exercises are not limited to the analysis step — they cover the entire incident lifecycle.

questions.text: Of what value is a Business Impact Analysis (BIA) in security incident response planning?
options: A. Identifies the business owners associated with information systems and therefore the escalation path
B. Identifies the systems that require forensic examination during an incident
C. Indirectly identifies the most important information systems that require protection from threats
D. Directly identifies the location of the most critical data
questions.justification_correct: The BIA identifies the most critical business processes in an organization and the information systems, service providers, and suppliers that support them. In security incident response planning, the BIA helps identify which systems are most important and therefore must be prioritized for protection and recovery.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because identifying business owners is a secondary benefit of the BIA. B is incorrect because the BIA does not determine the need for forensic examination. D is incorrect because the location of critical data is a by-product of the BIA but not its primary contribution to incident response planning.

questions.text: Threat analysts in an organization have identified a potential malware threat in an advisory. Detection in production systems will necessitate configuration changes to antivirus systems on production servers. What approach is best for making these configuration changes?
options: A. Make the changes as soon as possible on production servers to stop the threat.
B. Test the changes on nonproduction servers and measure performance impact.
C. Write a rule in intrusion detection systems to block the threat at the network layer.
D. Update antivirus signature files to permit detection of the threat.
questions.justification_correct: Testing configuration changes in a nonproduction environment is the best first step to ensure that changes will not adversely affect production system availability, transaction capacity, or correct function before deployment.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because deploying untested changes to production may cause unintended consequences including availability issues. C is incorrect because intrusion detection systems detect but cannot block intrusions. D is incorrect because threat analysts have already determined that configuration changes — not just signature updates — are necessary.

questions.text: Which methods are used to test security incident response plans?
options: A. Document review, tabletop simulation, actual incident
B. Document review, walkthrough, parallel test, cutover test
C. Document review, walkthrough, tabletop simulation, parallel test, cutover test
D. Document review, walkthrough, tabletop simulation
questions.justification_correct: The three types of tests for security incident response plans are: document review (individual review of the plan), walkthrough (group discussion of steps), and tabletop simulation (facilitated scenario-based exercise). These are distinct from disaster recovery testing methods.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because an actual incident is not a purposeful test method. B and C are incorrect because parallel test and cutover test are disaster recovery testing methods, not incident response testing methods.

questions.text: An organization discovers a data breach that may fall under multiple regulatory frameworks including GDPR. What should be the information security manager's FIRST priority when establishing incident response notification timelines?
options: A. Identify the most stringent notification requirement among applicable regulations
B. Notify all potentially affected regulatory bodies simultaneously within 24 hours
C. Wait for legal counsel to complete a full regulatory analysis before any notification
D. Follow the organization's standard 30-day incident response timeline
questions.justification_correct: The information security manager must first identify which regulations apply and determine the most stringent notification timeline requirement to ensure compliance with all applicable frameworks. Different regulations (GDPR, HIPAA, state breach laws, etc.) have varying notification windows, and the organization must meet the shortest deadline to remain compliant across all jurisdictions. This is a management-level decision that requires understanding the regulatory landscape and establishing appropriate incident response planning procedures. The ISM should have pre-established playbooks that identify applicable regulations based on data types and jurisdictions, enabling rapid determination of notification obligations during an actual incident.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because not all regulations require notification within 24 hours, and simultaneous notification without proper assessment may result in premature or inaccurate disclosures. Option C is incorrect because waiting for complete legal analysis may cause the organization to miss critical regulatory deadlines, resulting in non-compliance and penalties. Option D is incorrect because a standard 30-day timeline may exceed regulatory requirements (such as GDPR's 72-hour window) and demonstrates poor incident response planning that doesn't account for varying regulatory obligations.

questions.text: Ravila, a new CISO in a healthcare organization, is reviewing incident response records from the past several years. Ravila has determined that minor incidents were managed inconsistently from one incident to the next. Staff turnover has not been an issue. What is the most likely cause of this?
options: A. Insufficient capacity for storage of forensic evidence
B. Excessive meddling by executive management
C. Inattention to detail
D. Lack of detailed incident response playbooks
questions.justification_correct: The most likely cause of inconsistent responses to security incidents is the absence of detailed procedural documentation — commonly known as playbooks. Without playbooks, different responders may handle the same type of incident in different ways, leading to inconsistency.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because storage capacity issues would not cause inconsistent procedural responses. B is incorrect because executive interference is unlikely to produce systematic inconsistency across minor incidents. C is incorrect because inattention to detail is a symptom, not the root structural cause.

questions.text: Who are the best parties to develop an organization's security incident response plan?
options: A. Business leaders and the general counsel
B. Security consultants from an outside firm
C. Internal security specialists and technology subject matter experts
D. Regulators and security incident response subject matter experts
questions.justification_correct: Internal security specialists and technology subject matter experts are best positioned to develop an IRP because they have deep knowledge of the organization's specific business processes, technologies, and operational context — all of which are critical inputs to an effective plan.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because business leaders and legal counsel are key stakeholders but not the best developers of the plan. B is incorrect because outside consultants lack knowledge of internal processes and technology specifics. D is incorrect because regulators do not develop IRPs for the organizations they oversee.

questions.text: Which sequence correctly identifies the steps in security incident response?
options: A. Detection, analysis, containment, eradication, recovery, closure
B. Analysis, containment, eradication, recovery, closure
C. Detection, containment, closure, recovery
D. Detection, analysis, eradication, closure, recovery
questions.justification_correct: The correct sequence of steps in security incident response is: Detection, Analysis, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, and Closure. Each phase builds upon the previous one and must be completed in order for an effective and complete response.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because it omits the Detection phase. C is incorrect because it omits Analysis and Eradication and places Closure before Recovery. D is incorrect because it omits Containment and places Closure before Recovery.

questions.text: Designated incident responders would be asked to attend planned incident tabletop exercises for all reasons EXCEPT which one?
options: A. Tabletop exercises serve as training for incident responders.
B. Tabletop exercises are important for estimating the amount of time required to respond to security incidents.
C. Tabletop exercises help incident responders become familiar with incident response procedures.
D. Tabletop exercises help identify errors in incident response procedures.
questions.justification_correct: Tabletop exercises are generally not a suitable opportunity to estimate response time. They are designed to help responders become more familiar with procedures and to identify gaps or errors in the plan — not to measure timing.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because tabletop exercises do serve as training. C is incorrect because familiarization with procedures is a primary objective. D is incorrect because identifying errors in the IRP is a valid and important benefit of tabletop exercises.

questions.text: What is the best time frequency for conducting tabletop exercises?
options: A. When significant changes are made to the incident response plan
B. Annually
C. Annually, or when there are significant changes to the incident response plan
D. Upon inception of the initial incident response plan
questions.justification_correct: Incident response tabletop exercises should be performed at least annually and also when significant changes are made to the IRP. The onboarding of new incident responders may also present an appropriate additional opportunity.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because tabletop exercises should also be conducted annually even without changes. B is incorrect because annual exercises alone are insufficient if significant IRP changes occur between cycles. D is incorrect because a one-time exercise at inception is wholly inadequate for ongoing preparedness.

questions.text: Incident responders have been asked to review a newly developed incident response plan. Their feedback suggests confusion regarding what is expected from them and others in the organization during an actual incident. What is the most likely cause of this?
options: A. The incident response plan lacks definitions of roles and responsibilities.
B. The incident response plan lacks a list of key contacts.
C. The incident responders have not yet been trained in the use of the plan.
D. Outsourced forensics services have not yet been defined.
questions.justification_correct: When roles and responsibilities are undefined in an IRP, responders become confused about who is authorized to make key decisions — including incident declaration, escalation, and notification of regulators, affected parties, and law enforcement. This is a critical structural deficiency.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because the absence of key contacts would not produce this breadth of confusion. C is incorrect because lack of training is unlikely to cause uncertainty about who is responsible for specific activities. D is incorrect because the absence of a forensic firm would not cause the wider confusion described.

questions.text: A multinational organization developing its security incident response plan has created a severity level matrix based on data sensitivity, operational criticality, and data location. Why is this severity level scheme feasible or infeasible?
options: A. The scheme is feasible because it identifies basic characteristics of its data sets.
B. The scheme is feasible because of its simplicity.
C. The scheme is not feasible because of its complexity.
D. The scheme is not feasible because it is not mapped to business functions.
questions.justification_correct: The scheme is feasible because it addresses primary data characteristics: sensitivity (regulatory and contractual obligations), operational criticality (revenue and reputational impact), and location (local regulations and regional IR team identification). For a multinational, this complexity is appropriate and workable.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because this scheme is not simple — its feasibility lies in its relevance, not simplicity. C is incorrect because complexity alone does not make a scheme infeasible if it is logically structured. D is incorrect because there is insufficient information to conclude that business functions are not addressed.

questions.text: What is the main purpose for including an escalation process in an incident response plan?
options: A. Legal is notified only if regulators are required to be notified.
B. Executives are notified only if the incident is severe enough to warrant their involvement.
C. Legal is notified only if affected parties need to be notified.
D. It provides an additional set of resources to help the incident response team.
questions.justification_correct: The primary reason for escalation in an IRP is to provide a structured mechanism for specific executives to be notified during serious incidents. Severity levels define thresholds, communication frequencies, and named executives to contact, ensuring appropriate executive awareness of high-impact incidents.
questions.justification_wrong: A and C are incorrect because regulatory and legal notifications are narrow aspects of escalation, not its primary purpose. D is incorrect because escalation is severity-driven, not resource-driven — additional resources may be a by-product, not the primary objective.

questions.text: The entirety of a service provider contract on incident response states: 'Customer is to be notified within 48 hours of a suspected breach.' Why is this statement sufficient or insufficient?
options: A. The statement is insufficient because 'suspected breach' is ambiguous.
B. The statement is insufficient because 48 hours is too long an interval.
C. The statement is sufficient because 48 hours is considered reasonable.
D. The statement is sufficient because 'suspected breach' is a well-known industry term.
questions.justification_correct: The term 'suspected breach' is ambiguous and undefined, making it possible for an organization to classify nearly any activity as a 'suspected breach' or, conversely, to avoid triggering notification. The term must be clearly defined to make the contractual obligation enforceable and meaningful.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because 48 hours is a reasonable and widely used notification interval. C is incorrect because while 48 hours is reasonable, the ambiguity of 'suspected breach' still makes the statement insufficient. D is incorrect because 'suspected breach' is not consistently interpreted across the industry.

questions.text: An organization has successfully completed training and walkthroughs of its incident response plan. What is the next best step?
options: A. Repeat training at regular intervals.
B. Conduct one or more tabletop exercises.
C. Wait for an actual incident to prove the effectiveness of training and walkthroughs.
D. Conduct a penetration test of production systems to measure response.
questions.justification_correct: After completing IRP training and walkthroughs, the next logical step is to conduct tabletop exercises — facilitated simulations of actual incidents that test whether the IRP is effective and that help responders practice their roles in a realistic but controlled scenario.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because repeating training is not the next best step after walkthroughs — progression to tabletop exercises is. C is incorrect because waiting for an actual incident without further preparation is irresponsible. D is incorrect because a penetration test identifies vulnerabilities, not incident response capability.

questions.text: In a business-to-business service provider contract, which language is most reasonable for notification of a security incident?
options: A. Notify customer within 1 hour of a breach
B. Notify customer within 48 hours of a suspected breach
C. Notify customer immediately after a breach
D. Notify customer within 48 hours of a breach
questions.justification_correct: Notification within 48 hours of a confirmed breach is the most reasonable contractual language. The term 'breach' should be clearly defined elsewhere in the contract. This timeframe balances the need for timely notification with the practical realities of incident investigation.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because 1 hour is unreasonably fast for notification after a breach. B is incorrect because 'suspected breach' is ambiguous and creates potential for misuse. C is incorrect because 'immediately' is both ambiguous and often operationally unrealistic.

questions.text: Under what circumstances would a security incident be accompanied by the triggering of a disaster recovery plan?
options: A. When the RPO has been exceeded
B. When the IRP calls out the DRP
C. When the security incident renders the system unusable
D. When a disaster has also occurred
questions.justification_correct: A disaster recovery plan may need to be triggered after a security incident if one or more systems become incapacitated — whether due to attacker activity, system damage, or the need to take a system offline for forensic analysis. The DR plan is activated when normal system operation cannot continue.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because RPO is a data recovery target, not a DR activation trigger. B is incorrect because the IRP should reference the DRP as guidance, but this alone does not trigger DR activation. D is incorrect because a separate declared disaster is not required to trigger a DRP during a security incident.

questions.text: During a suspected service disruption event, the network operations team reports that traffic volumes have exceeded normal capacity. As the information security manager, what should be your FIRST priority?
options: A. Determine whether the event meets the criteria for formal incident declaration and activate the incident response plan
B. Direct the network team to implement traffic filtering rules to block suspicious source addresses
C. Contact the internet service provider to request additional bandwidth capacity
D. Initiate the business continuity plan and prepare to activate alternate processing sites
questions.justification_correct: As an information security manager, the first priority is to assess whether the event meets organizational thresholds for incident declaration. This management decision determines the appropriate response framework, escalation procedures, stakeholder notification requirements, and resource allocation. Formal incident declaration triggers documented response procedures, ensures proper governance, and establishes the incident command structure necessary for coordinated response. This is a strategic decision that affects business operations, legal obligations, and regulatory compliance, making it the appropriate first management action before directing specific technical or business continuity responses.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is a technical implementation task that should be executed by technical teams after management has declared an incident and authorized response actions. Option C addresses a potential technical solution but bypasses the critical management decision of incident classification and may not be appropriate for all scenarios (e.g., if this is a deliberate attack rather than capacity issue). Option D escalates too quickly to business continuity without first assessing the incident scope and severity—business continuity/disaster recovery activation is a significant management decision that should follow proper incident assessment and may not be warranted for all service disruptions.

questions.text: During a significant service disruption affecting customer-facing systems, the information security manager must decide on the appropriate response classification. What should be the PRIMARY factor in determining whether to declare a formal security incident?
options: A. The technical attack vector and methodology used by the threat actor
B. The potential impact to business operations and regulatory obligations
C. The availability of forensic evidence for law enforcement prosecution
D. The capacity of the security operations center to investigate the event
questions.justification_correct: The PRIMARY factor in declaring a formal security incident from a management perspective is the potential impact to business operations and regulatory obligations. This aligns with CISM's focus on business-driven security management. The declaration decision should be based on business risk assessment, including operational impact, data confidentiality concerns, regulatory reporting requirements, and potential reputational damage. This ensures appropriate escalation to senior management, activation of incident response procedures, and compliance with notification obligations. The incident declaration triggers resource allocation, stakeholder communication, and potential invocation of business continuity plans based on business impact severity.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the technical attack methodology is an investigation detail, not the primary declaration criterion—management focuses on business impact regardless of attack type. Option C is incorrect because law enforcement involvement and prosecution potential are secondary considerations that follow business impact assessment and may not be relevant for all incidents. Option D is incorrect because resource availability should not determine whether an incident is declared; instead, declaration based on business impact should drive resource allocation decisions and potentially trigger external support engagement.

questions.text: Which of the following is the best description of the kill chain?
options: A. A teaching tool that helps people understand a cyberattack
B. A model that depicts a typical attack scenario
C. A method used by wiper malware
D. A method used by malware droppers
questions.justification_correct: The kill chain is a model that depicts the stages of a typical attack scenario: target identification, selection and deployment of forces, attack on target, and accomplishment of the attack objective. It is used in threat intelligence and incident response to understand and disrupt attacker progression.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because the kill chain is more than a teaching tool — it is a realistic model used operationally. C and D are incorrect because the kill chain is not a type of malware behavior; it is a strategic framework for understanding the attack lifecycle.

questions.text: When is the best time to develop an incident response plan?
options: A. At the start of the next budget cycle
B. As soon as possible
C. When an attack is suspected
D. When an attack is confirmed
questions.justification_correct: An IRP should be developed as soon as possible because attacks can occur at any time. Waiting for a budget cycle, a suspected attack, or a confirmed attack is irresponsible — preparation must precede the event to be effective.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because waiting for a future budget cycle creates unnecessary exposure. C and D are incorrect because an IRP developed during an active attack has no value — the plan must be in place, tested, and practiced before an incident occurs.

questions.text: When is the best time to declare a security incident?
options: A. At the start of the next budget cycle
B. As soon as possible
C. When an attack is suspected
D. When an attack is confirmed
questions.justification_correct: A security incident should be declared when an attack is suspected, not confirmed. Early declaration triggers investigative activities that may lead to confirmation and enables earlier containment, reducing potential damage. Waiting for confirmation can allow the attacker more time to cause harm.
questions.justification_wrong: A and B are incorrect because incident declaration should be event-driven, not time-based. D is incorrect because waiting for confirmation unnecessarily delays the activation of incident response procedures and extends attacker dwell time.

questions.text: Organizations should outsource security incident response for all of the following reasons EXCEPT:
options: A. Difficulty justifying the hire of qualified incident responders
B. Difficulty estimating the likelihood or impact of incidents
C. Difficulty affording the compensation for incident responders
D. Difficulty justifying the hire of workers with specialized skills
questions.justification_correct: Difficulty estimating the likelihood or impact of incidents is a risk assessment challenge, not a reason to outsource incident response. This difficulty applies equally whether IR is insourced or outsourced and does not drive the outsourcing decision.
questions.justification_wrong: A, C, and D are all incorrect because they are all valid reasons why organizations choose to outsource IR — cost of qualified staff, budget constraints, and specialized skill requirements are legitimate drivers for outsourcing decisions.

questions.text: What is the best method for a new security leader to examine an organization's security incident response plan?
options: A. Hire an external auditor to examine the plan
B. Interview incident responders and solicit their opinion
C. Conduct a walkthrough and measure the results
D. Perform a gap analysis to compare the existing plan with the ideal plan
questions.justification_correct: A gap analysis is the most effective approach for a new security leader to assess an IRP. By comparing the existing plan against an ideal or standard framework, the leader can objectively identify deficiencies, prioritize improvements, and establish a baseline for future maturity measurement.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because external audit is unnecessary and costly for an internal plan assessment. B is incorrect because incident responders may not have sufficient perspective to assess plan adequacy comprehensively. C is incorrect because a walkthrough alone may not reveal all improvement areas compared to a structured gap analysis.

questions.text: What criteria should be used to select the types of incidents chosen for playbook development?
options: A. High-impact incidents least likely to occur
B. High-impact incidents most likely to occur
C. Incidents in scope of applicable regulations
D. Incidents that receive the most publicity
questions.justification_correct: Playbooks should be developed for high-impact incidents that are most likely to occur, such as ransomware, business email compromise, and credential theft. This combination of likelihood and impact ensures that playbook investments deliver maximum risk reduction value.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because low-likelihood events, even if high-impact, do not warrant playbook investment as a primary focus. C is incorrect because regulatory scope is not the primary driver for playbook development. D is incorrect because publicity is not a valid criterion — many high-impact incidents receive little public attention.

questions.text: An organization is conducting a study to identify its most critical business processes. What is this study commonly known as?
options: A. Business impact analysis
B. Enterprise risk assessment
C. Vulnerability assessment
D. Enterprise gap assessment
questions.justification_correct: A business impact analysis (BIA) is the study conducted in business continuity planning to identify the most critical business processes, their dependencies, and the impact of their disruption on the organization's operations, revenue, and reputation.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because an enterprise risk assessment identifies and evaluates risks, not critical process prioritization specifically. C is incorrect because a vulnerability assessment identifies weaknesses in systems and processes. D is incorrect because a gap assessment compares current state against a standard or ideal state.

questions.text: 'Accounts payable and accounts receivable functions will be unable to process, impacting the availability of services and supplies and resulting in reduced revenue' is an example of which of the following?
options: A. An SLA
B. A consequence
C. A statement of criticality
D. A statement of impact
questions.justification_correct: This is a statement of impact, which is a component of the BIA that describes the business consequence of the loss or disruption of a critical asset or process in operational and financial terms. It translates technical disruptions into business language.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because an SLA defines service performance commitments, not disruption consequences. B is incorrect because while this describes a consequence, 'statement of impact' is the more precise and correct BIA terminology. C is incorrect because a statement of criticality ranks the importance of assets, not their disruption consequences.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a criticality analysis?
options: A. A study of the vulnerabilities of a system or process
B. A study of the impact of the incapacitation of a system or process
C. A statement describing the consequences of the loss of a system or process
D. A study of the dependencies of a system or process
questions.justification_correct: A criticality analysis is a specialized risk assessment focused on understanding the threats, vulnerabilities, and business impact associated with the disruption of a specific system or process, particularly in the context of business continuity and disaster recovery planning.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because a criticality analysis includes vulnerability assessment but is broader in scope. C is incorrect because a statement of consequences is a specific output called a Statement of Impact. D is incorrect because dependency analysis is a component of the BIA, not the criticality analysis specifically.

questions.text: Which of the following is the best definition of maximum tolerable downtime?
options: A. The time since disaster onset in which the organization's survival is at risk
B. The time in which the organization is required to disclose a breach
C. The time since disaster onset in which the organization must recover
D. The time in which an organization initiates a disaster recovery plan
questions.justification_correct: Maximum tolerable downtime (MTD), also known as acceptable interruption window (AIW), is the theoretical maximum period from disaster onset beyond which the organization's continued survival is at risk. It is the upper bound for all recovery time objectives.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because MTD is not related to breach disclosure requirements. C is incorrect because MTD is the threshold of survival risk, not a specific recovery deadline. D is incorrect because DR plan initiation timing is separate from the MTD concept.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the metric maximum tolerable outage?
options: A. The dwell time between the onset and the declaration of a disaster
B. The theoretical period of time since the onset of a disaster in which the organization's survival is at risk
C. The longest that an organization can operate in emergency operations mode
D. The greatest allowable loss of data in a disaster or breach scenario
questions.justification_correct: Maximum tolerable outage (MTO) is the longest period an organization can operate in emergency operations (disaster recovery) mode before normal production operations must be restored. MTO drives the planning requirement to reestablish normal operations within a defined window.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because MTO is not a dwell time measure. B is incorrect because this definition describes maximum tolerable downtime (MTD), not MTO. D is incorrect because maximum data loss is expressed as recovery point objective (RPO).

questions.text: Which of the following is the best description of the greatest amount of acceptable data loss in a disaster scenario?
options: A. Recovery time objective
B. Recovery point objective
C. Service delivery objective
D. Maximum tolerable downtime
questions.justification_correct: The recovery point objective (RPO) defines the maximum amount of data loss an organization can tolerate, measured as the point in time to which data must be restored. It directly drives backup frequency and replication requirements.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because RTO measures recovery time, not data loss. C is incorrect because SDO measures the level of service quality at a recovery site. D is incorrect because MTD measures the time threshold for organizational survival, not data loss.

questions.text: Which position is responsible for determining RPO and RTO recovery targets?
options: A. Business continuity planner
B. Disaster recovery planner
C. IT architect
D. Business unit leader
questions.justification_correct: Recovery targets including RPO and RTO are established by the business unit leader or department head responsible for the key business processes in question. They are best positioned to understand tolerable downtime and data loss from an operational and revenue perspective.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because BC planners facilitate the process but do not set the targets. B is incorrect because DR planners design solutions to meet targets set by business leaders. C is incorrect because IT architects design technical architectures based on targets set by business units.

questions.text: Disaster recovery planners and management have agreed that the acceptable throughput of a system in emergency operations mode can be one-half of the capacity of the primary system. This is expressed as:
options: A. Recovery capacity objective
B. Recovery point objective
C. Service delivery objective
D. Recovery time objective
questions.justification_correct: The recovery capacity objective (RCapO) expresses the acceptable transaction throughput or processing capacity of a recovery system as a proportion of the primary system's capacity. In this case, 50% capacity is the agreed RCapO.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because RPO measures maximum acceptable data loss, not throughput. C is incorrect because SDO measures service quality level, which overlaps conceptually but RCapO is the more specific and correct term for throughput capacity. D is incorrect because RTO measures time to resume service.

questions.text: For a given cost level, a disaster recovery planner has determined that the best achievable RTO for a system is 12 hours, even though management has set the RTO at 4 hours. What is the disaster recovery planner's next step?
options: A. Inform management that the desired RTO can be met within budget.
B. Change the RTO from 12 hours to 4 hours.
C. Change the RTO from 4 hours to 12 hours.
D. Inform management that the desired RTO cannot be achieved within budget.
questions.justification_correct: When recovery targets cannot be met within the allocated budget, the DR planner must inform management of the gap. Management must then decide whether to increase the budget to achieve the desired RTO or adjust the RTO to align with available resources.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because the scenario explicitly states the 4-hour RTO cannot be achieved at the given cost. B and C are incorrect because DR planners do not have authority to unilaterally change recovery targets — that authority belongs to business and executive management.

questions.text: The architecture of an alternate processing system to be used in the event of a disaster is best determined by:
options: A. Management
B. BC planners
C. DR planners
D. IT architects
questions.justification_correct: Once recovery targets (RPO, RTO) have been established by business management, the architecture of the alternate processing system should be designed by IT architects. They have the technical expertise to translate business recovery requirements into viable technical solutions.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because management sets recovery targets but does not design technical systems. B and C are incorrect because BC and DR planners coordinate the planning effort but are not the appropriate parties to design the technical architecture of recovery systems.

questions.text: For disaster recovery purposes, why is book value NOT a preferred method for determining the value of assets?
options: A. Information assets have no book value.
B. Book value may vary based on location if a recovery site is located elsewhere.
C. Some assets may not be tracked for depreciation.
D. The cost to replace damaged or destroyed assets could exceed book value.
questions.justification_correct: For disaster recovery, replacement cost is the preferred valuation method because damaged or destroyed assets must be replaced at current market prices, which may be significantly higher than depreciated book value. Using book value could result in inadequate funding for actual replacement needs.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because this question is not limited to information assets. B is incorrect because this is not a true or relevant statement about book value and location. C is incorrect because while true that some assets may not be depreciated, this is not the primary reason book value is unsuitable for DR purposes.

questions.text: For disaster recovery scenarios, which of the following methods for setting the value of computer equipment is most appropriate?
options: A. Recovery cost
B. Replacement cost
C. Lost revenue
D. Book value
questions.justification_correct: Replacement cost is most appropriate for disaster recovery scenarios involving computer equipment. In a disaster, equipment that is damaged or destroyed must be replaced at current market prices, making replacement cost the most realistic and actionable valuation method.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because recovery cost is more associated with data recovery than physical equipment. C is incorrect because lost revenue may be relevant for business interruption insurance but is not an equipment valuation method. D is incorrect because book value (depreciated cost) may be insufficient to fund actual replacement of damaged equipment.

questions.text: How are security requirements integrated into disaster recovery plans?
options: A. Security requirements and controls are a part of the foundation of DR plans and capabilities.
B. Management selects the most important security controls and requirements to be a part of DR.
C. The purpose of DR is different from cybersecurity and the two are not related.
D. Only those controls required by law are a part of DR plans and capabilities.
questions.justification_correct: All of an organization's security policies, requirements, and controls apply equally to all environments — primary or recovery. When processing shifts to a DR facility, that facility effectively becomes the primary processing environment and all security controls must remain in force.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because security requirements cannot be selectively applied based on management preference — all applicable controls must be maintained. C is incorrect because cybersecurity and DR are deeply interrelated. D is incorrect because organizations may have security obligations beyond legal requirements that must also apply to DR environments.

questions.text: What is the best approach to the development of an organization's security incident response plan?
options: A. Developing separate security incident recordkeeping
B. Developing a general IR plan and leaving the details to subject matter experts
C. Developing detailed playbooks and relying on the organization's crisis management plan
D. Leveraging the organization's crisis management plan
questions.justification_correct: The best approach is to leverage existing organizational processes wherever possible, including the corporate crisis management plan, IT incident response processes, and emergency communications plans. Building upon existing frameworks is more effective and efficient than creating separate parallel processes.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because separate recordkeeping creates silos — existing systems should be leveraged. B is incorrect because detailed playbooks must also be developed, not left entirely to SME discretion. C is incorrect because detailed playbooks are needed, but an overall IRP framework must also be developed alongside the crisis management plan.

questions.text: Why should an organization's crisis management plan be leveraged when developing a security incident response plan?
options: A. The crisis management plan contains contact information for regulators.
B. The crisis management plan contains details on the recovery of information systems.
C. It is more efficient to leverage an existing process than to build a new one.
D. The crisis management plan contains definitions for RTO and RPO.
questions.justification_correct: When building an IRP, leveraging existing organizational capabilities and processes — such as the crisis management plan — is far more effective and efficient than building a parallel process from scratch. It avoids duplication of effort and ensures consistency across organizational response frameworks.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because a crisis management plan is unlikely to contain regulator contact information. B is incorrect because system recovery details are in DR plans, not crisis management plans. D is incorrect because RTO and RPO definitions are in DR plans, not crisis management plans.

questions.text: Which of the following is the best reason for an organization to maintain an emergency communications plan?
options: A. To ensure that the organization maintains communications with its customers during an incident
B. To ensure that the organization has a method for notifying regulators of a breach
C. To ensure that the organization can communicate effectively during a disaster
D. To ensure that the organization can communicate internally and externally during a disaster or high-severity incident
questions.justification_correct: An emergency communications plan (ECP) ensures that the organization has structured, prearranged methods for communicating both internally (with employees, management, and executives) and externally (with customers, suppliers, regulators, media, and the public) during a disaster or high-severity security incident.
questions.justification_wrong: A, B, and C are all incorrect because they each represent only a narrow aspect of the emergency communications plan's purpose. The ECP covers all stakeholder groups — internal and external — and applies to both disasters and high-severity security incidents.

questions.text: While a severe security incident is in progress, the CISO wants to provide an update to executive management. What is the best method for providing this update?
options: A. Send an encrypted e-mail message to all executives.
B. Post an update on the organization's intranet site.
C. Use the communication method prescribed in the emergency communications plan.
D. Call a meeting of all executives in the main conference room.
questions.justification_correct: During a high-severity incident or disaster, all communications should follow the methods and protocols defined in the organization's emergency communications plan. This ensures that updates are consistent, authorized, and delivered through prearranged channels appropriate to the situation.
questions.justification_wrong: A is incorrect because encrypted email may not be received or read in a timely manner during an active incident. B is incorrect because intranet posts may expose sensitive incident details to unauthorized personnel or potentially the attacker. D is incorrect because a physical meeting may be impractical and unnecessarily delays communications.

questions.text: An organization has experienced a ransomware attack that has encrypted several production servers. The incident response team has declared a high-severity incident. Who should be responsible for notifying the media?
options: A. The CISO
B. The incident response team leader
C. The CEO
D. The designated public relations spokesperson
questions.justification_correct: The organization's emergency communications plan should designate a specific public relations spokesperson or team to handle all communications with the media and the public. This ensures that all statements are accurate, legally reviewed, and consistent with management guidance.
questions.justification_wrong: A, B, and C are all incorrect because none of these individuals should communicate directly with the media unless they have been explicitly designated as the public relations spokesperson for the incident in the emergency communications plan.

questions.text: Why is an incident response plan alone insufficient for an organization to manage security incidents effectively?
options: A. An IRP does not contain the technical procedures needed to respond to specific types of attacks.
B. An IRP is only used for low-severity incidents.
C. An IRP does not define the roles and responsibilities of the incident response team.
D. An IRP is not authorized by executive management.
questions.justification_correct: An IRP provides the high-level framework — roles, responsibilities, escalation paths, and communication protocols — but lacks the specific, step-by-step technical procedures required to respond to distinct attack types such as ransomware, DDoS, or phishing. These detailed procedures are contained in incident response playbooks.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because an IRP applies to incidents of all severity levels. C is incorrect because a well-developed IRP does define roles and responsibilities. D is incorrect because an IRP must always be authorized by executive management as a foundational requirement.
